planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after last frost in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; cork-stemmed passionflower is native to the tropical Americas and naturalized throughout the Gulf South, where it grows in disturbed habitats, forest margins, and coastal thickets.

directions: Plant in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; cork-stemmed passionflower is a compact, small-flowered passionflower with distinctively corky, ridged stems that distinguish it from other species. The tiny greenish-white to cream flowers are inconspicuous but produce small, dark purple to black berries that are important food for white-eyed vireos, gray catbirds, and other frugivorous birds. Most significantly, it is an essential larval host plant for multiple Gulf fritillary, zebra longwing, and Julia heliconian butterfly species — its small, tri-lobed leaves are consumed voraciously by caterpillars. In zone 9a, it is a reliable perennial vine that provides critical butterfly habitat with almost no maintenance.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; snail vine grows rapidly in warm conditions and may re-sprout from the root in zone 9 after light frosts. Train on a trellis or fence.

directions: Direct sow after frost in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; snail vine is a vigorous tropical vine from South America producing some of the most uniquely shaped flowers in the legume family — the petals are twisted into a tight spiral or snail-shell shape, creating a flower that looks as if it has been wound up like a corkscrew. The flowers are lavender-purple to pink and emit a rich, honey-vanilla fragrance that intensifies in the afternoon and evening. A rapid grower once established, covering a trellis or fence with lush, tropical foliage and weeks of unusual, fragrant flowers from midsummer through fall. Thomas Jefferson famously grew snail vine at Monticello, where he valued it as an ornamental and edible bean — the pods and seeds are edible. In zone 9a, it may perennialize in warm microclimates.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in dry to well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic soil in full sun; pink wild bean is a native annual vine of sandy, coastal, and inland dry habitats, fixing nitrogen and providing important wildlife food.

directions: Direct sow in dry, sandy, slightly acidic soil in full sun; pink wild bean is a native twining annual legume of dry, sandy coastal plain habitats from Long Island to Florida and Texas, producing small, rose-pink to pinkish-lavender flowers typical of wild beans followed by narrow seed pods. The seeds are eaten by bobwhite quail and other ground-foraging birds, making it a valuable wildlife plant for naturalistic meadow and coastal garden settings. As a native nitrogen-fixing legume, it improves soil fertility while providing summer flowering interest. The plant twines readily through other vegetation or on a small trellis. In zone 9a, it grows rapidly as a summer annual from seed and self-sows to perpetuate itself in appropriate dry, sandy soils.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants on a very strong support structure in spring; Japanese wisteria is vigorous and heavy — supports must be extremely sturdy. Prune twice yearly (summer and winter) to control size and encourage flowering. May take 3–7 years to bloom from seed or cutting.

directions: Plant on an extremely sturdy support structure in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Japanese wisteria is considered by many gardeners the most ornamental of the wisterias, producing extraordinarily long (up to 36 inches), pendant flower racemes in shades from white through lilac, violet, and purple — cascading down in waves of color in spring. The fragrance is sweet and pervasive. However, it is the most aggressive and potentially invasive of the wisterias in the American South (it is invasive in many southeastern states) — the native alternatives (W. frutescens, W. macrostachya) are strongly recommended for environmental responsibility. If growing Japanese wisteria, regular biannual pruning is essential to contain the plant and promote flowering. NOTE: all wisteria parts are toxic if ingested; the seeds particularly so.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock in spring in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; evergreen wisteria is a Chinese legume vine suited for zones 8–10, where it retains its leaves year-round unlike deciduous wisterias.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; evergreen wisteria is a Chinese legume vine in the same family as wisteria, producing dark, wine-purple to reddish-purple flower clusters from midsummer through fall — a very different bloom time from the spring-flowering true wisterias. The pinnate leaves are glossy and retain their attractive appearance year-round in mild climates. Compared to the aggressive true wisterias, Millettia reticulata is more restrained and less invasive, making it somewhat more manageable in the garden. The deep purple to burgundy flower clusters have a spicy, grapelike fragrance. An excellent vine for providing summer-through-fall fragrant flowering interest on arbors and pergolas in zone 9a gardens, where it is reliably perennial.
